title: Arrhythmia — Pulseless Ventricular Tachycardia
oneLiner: Shockable cardiac arrest rhythm from disorganized ventricular electrical activity; occurs post-MI, in cardiomyopathy, or channelopathies; requires immediate defibrillation.

sections.Pulseless Ventricular Tachycardia (pVT): **One-liner:** Shockable cardiac arrest rhythm from disorganized ventricular electrical activity; occurs post-MI, in cardiomyopathy, or channelopathies; requires immediate defibrillation.
sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:** A 62-year-old man, 3 days post-anterior ST-elevation MI, collapses on the ward. Nursing staff find him unresponsive, not breathing, and no pulse. The cardiac monitor initially shows a run of wide-complex tachycardia at 200/min, then degenerates into a coarse, sinusoidal waveform. Resuscitation trolley arrives; rhythm check reveals no output. He had complained of chest tightness and palpitations 20 minutes earlier.

**Dissecting the vignette:**
- **Post-MI setting** → scarred myocardium creates re-entry circuits; highest risk in first 48-72 hours
- **Collapse and no pulse** → hemodynamically unstable; must distinguish from perfusing VT (which has a pulse)
- **Wide-complex tachycardia before arrest** → ventricular origin; QRS >120 ms, no visible P waves
- **Coarse sinusoidal waveform** → characteristic pattern of pVT; amplitude often >5 mm
- **Chest tightness and palpitations** → prodomal ischemia triggers ectopy; may herald VT storm

**Atypical presentations:**
- **Torsades de Pointes (TdP):** Polymorphic pVT with twisting QRS axis; often drug-induced (e.g., sotalol), hypomagnesemia, or congenital long QT. On monitor: sinusoidal amplitude waxes and wanes.
- **Fine pVT:** Low-amplitude, almost flat line → easily mistaken for asystole. Switch leads or gain; look for slow, irregular undulations. Must treat as pVT until proven otherwise.
- **pVT in young athlete:** No MI history; suspect hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, myocarditis, or commotio cordis (blow to chest). Presentation: sudden collapse during exertion.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:** Pulseless ventricular tachycardia is a rapid, organized ventricular rhythm (≥120 bpm) that fails to generate a palpable pulse. The heart contracts too fast and ineffectively → no cardiac output → cardiac arrest.

**Why it matters:**
- **Frequency:** ~20% of in-hospital cardiac arrests are shockable rhythms (pVT or VF). pVT is the most common initial rhythm in out-of-hospital cardiac arrest with known cardiac disease.
- **Stakes:** Defibrillation is the only effective treatment; each minute delay reduces survival by 7-10%. Mortality >85% without prompt intervention.
- **Common pitfalls:**
  - Mistaking pVT for asystole (fine pVT) → delays shocking → worse outcome.
  - Trying to check a pulse during rhythm check → waste time; if no pulse → shock.
  - Forgetting to treat underlying cause (e.g., acute coronary syndrome, electrolyte disturbance) → recurrence.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**Story of development:**
1. **Trigger:** A premature ventricular complex (PVC) falls on the vulnerable period of repolarization (R-on-T phenomenon). This initiates a re-entry circuit around scar tissue, ischemia border zone, or electrolyte imbalance.
2. **Re-entry:** A unidirectional block in one limb of the circuit allows the wavefront to circle back and re-excite tissue. This self-perpetuating loop causes rapid ventricular firing (150-250 bpm).
3. **Loss of output:** At such high rates, ventricular filling time collapses → stroke volume drops → no pulse. The atria continue to contract but against a closed AV valve → no atrial kick.

**Mechanism → sign:**
- **Rapid wide QRS** → ventricular origin; conduction bypasses His-Purkinje system → slow cell-to-cell conduction → broad QRS (usually >140 ms)
- **No P waves** → sinus node cannot compete; sometimes retrograde P waves buried in QRS or T wave (telltale sign if visible)
- **Monomorphic pVT** → single re-entry circuit (e.g., around MI scar); QRS shape constant
- **Polymorphic pVT** → multiple circuits or changing substrate (e.g., ischemia, electrolyte flux); QRS morphology varies beat-to-beat; think TdP if twisting axis
- **Coarse vs fine** → amplitude reflects mass of contracting myocardium; coarse means more viable muscle – more likely to respond to defibrillation

**Connection to pattern cues:**
- Post-MI vignette → re-entry around scar
- Young athlete → structural heart disease (HCM) or blunt trauma (commotio) causes ventricular irritability
- Torsades → triggered activity due to prolonged repolarization (long QT) – not re-entry
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Common Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|--------------|-----------|
| **Ischaemic** | Acute MI (STEMI > NSTEMI) | Chest pain, ST elevation, troponin rise |
| **Structural** | Cardiomyopathy (ischaemic, dilated, hypertrophic) | Known LV dysfunction, heart failure |
| **Electrolyte** | Hypokalaemia, hypomagnesaemia, hyperkalaemia | Diuretic use, renal failure, prolonged QT |
| **Drug/toxin** | Antiarrhythmics (IC – flecainide, III – sotalol/amiodarone) | Induction of torsades; preceding PVCs |
| **Channelopathy** | Long QT syndrome, Brugada syndrome | Family history of sudden death, syncope |
| **Electrical** | Commotio cordis, electrocution | Blunt chest trauma (sports), electrical injury |
| **Inflammatory** | Myocarditis (viral, autoimmune) | Fever, recent viral illness, chest pain |
| **Other** | Hypothermia, hypoxia, acidosis | Environmental exposure, sepsis, respiratory failure |

**Most common cause:** Acute myocardial infarction (especially anterior STEMI) – responsible for ~50-70% of pVT arrests.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria:** pVT is defined by: absence of pulse + monomorphic or polymorphic wide QRS tachycardia (≥120 bpm) on cardiac monitor or ECG. No further scoring needed; it's a rhythm diagnosis in cardiac arrest.

**Key tests (all during arrest or immediately post-ROSC):**
- **Rhythm check (paddles/monitor):** Shows wide QRS >120 bpm, no pulse. **Single finding confirms diagnosis** – proceed to defibrillation.
- **12-lead ECG (post-ROSC):** Look for ischaemia (ST elevation/depression), QT prolongation, Brugada pattern, or evidence of underlying structural disease. **Pattern:** If QT >500 ms in sinus rhythm, suspect TdP risk.
- **Arterial blood gas (ABG):** Assess oxygenation, acidosis, potassium, magnesium. **Interpretation:** Severe acidosis (pH <7.0) impairs defibrillation success; correct K+ <3.5 or >5.5, Mg <1.8 mg/dL (0.74 mmol/L).
- **Troponin:** Post-arrest – elevated due to ischaemia (either cause or consequence). Serial trend helps distinguish primary MI vs arrest-related damage.
- **Point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS):** During pulse check – if rhythm looks like pVT but POCUS shows coordinated ventricular contraction → pseudo-PEA (treat underlying cause). **Rules out pVT if organized contraction seen.**
- **ECG during stable VT (if perfusing):** For structurally normal hearts, VT can be idiopathic (RVOT, fascicular). But if pulseless → treat as pVT regardless.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour):** Follow ALS algorithm.
- **Call for help and defibrillator.** Start CPR with hard and fast compressions (100-120/min, depth 5-6 cm), 30:2 ratio.
- **Defibrillate** *as soon as rhythm confirmed pVT.* Use biphasic 150-200 J (monophasic 360 J). If unsure pVT vs asystole → give shock (low risk, high benefit).
- **After each shock:** Immediately resume CPR for 2 min, then rhythm check. Do not delay CPR to check pulse.
- **If shock fails (refractory pVT):**
  - **Amiodarone 300 mg IV/IO** (first dose) followed by 5% dextrose flush. After 3-5 shocks if still pVT → second dose amiodarone 150 mg IV/IO.
  - Alternative: **Lidocaine 1-1.5 mg/kg IV/IO** (max 3 mg/kg). Use if amiodarone contraindicated (e.g., known thyroid disease, prolonged QT on prior ECG).
  - **Magnesium 2 g IV/IO** if Torsades de Pointes suspected (polymorphic pVT with long QT on pre-arrest ECG).
- **Treat reversible causes (4H & 4T):** Hypoxia, Hypovolaemia, Hypo/hyperkalaemia, Hypothermia; Tension pneumothorax, Tamponade, Toxins, Thrombosis (coronary/pulmonary). These explain persistent pVT despite shocks.
- **If return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC):** Post-cardiac arrest care – controlled oxygenation, target MAP >65 mmHg, therapeutic hypothermia, urgent PCI if STEMI.

**Ward (24-72h):**
- **Continuous ECG monitoring** in CCU/ICU. Watch for recurrent pVT or VT storm.
- **Correct electrolytes:** K+ >4.0, Mg >1.0 mmol/L, Ca++ adjusted normal.
- **Identify and treat acute coronary syndrome:** Urgent angiography ± revascularisation.
- **If frequent PVCs/short runs:** consider beta-blocker (e.g., metoprolol 50 mg PO BD) to suppress triggers.
- **Amiodarone loading:** 200 mg PO TDS for 1 week, then 200 mg daily for maintenance (only if not already given IV loading).

**Long-term:**
- **ICD implantation** (secondary prevention) in survivors with structural heart disease (LVEF ≤35%) or genetic channelopathies.
- **Optimise heart failure therapy** (ACEi, beta-blocker, spironolactone) to reduce arrhythmia recurrence.
- **Avoid pro-arrhythmic drugs** (especially class IC agents like flecainide in structural disease).
- **Education:** Patient & family – when to call 999/emergency services for palpitations or syncope.

**Common pitfalls:**
- **Do NOT** give vasopressin (ineffective) or amiodarine after asystole/PEA (no benefit, may lower BP).
- **Do NOT** delay shock to intubate or get IV access. Shock first, then access.
- **Do NOT** use paddle-to-skin contact with gel – use defibrillator pads correctly to avoid burns.
- **Do NOT** stop CPR for more than 5-10 seconds during rhythm check.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Pulseless VT | Ventricular Fibrillation (VF) | Asystole | Perfusing VT (pVT with pulse) |
|---------|--------------|-------------------------------|----------|------------------------------|
| **Pulse** | Absent | Absent | Absent | Present (weak) |
| **ECG (rhythm)** | Wide QRS complexes (regular) | Chaotic, no clear QRS | Flat line | Wide QRS, regular, rate 100-150 |
| **Amplitude** | Coarse (≥2 mm) or fine | Usually coarse; fine VF <2 mm | No amplitude | Variable |
| **Tachycardia** | Yes (≥120 bpm) | Can be rapid but unorganised | No | Yes (120-200 bpm) |
| **QRS morphology** | Monomorphic or polymorphic | None (fibrillatory waves) | None | Monomorphic/polymorphic |
| **Treatment** | Defibrillate, amiodarone | Defibrillate, amiodarone | CPR, adrenaline (epinephrine) | If unstable → cardioversion; if stable → amiodarone/electrophysiology |

**Key differentiator:** Perfusing VT has a pulse – check central pulse (carotid/femoral) for up to 10 seconds. If unsure, treat as pulseless.
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
- **Failure to shock immediately:** Account for each minute delay → doubling of mortality. After 10 minutes, survival <2%. Brain injury from prolonged hypoxia.
- **Recurrent pVT storm:** Multiple shocks may cause myocardial stunning, hypotension, and electrical myocardial depression. Management: sedation (propofol), revascularisation, deep sedation with beta-blocker ± overdrive pacing.
- **Post-arrest syndrome:** Global ischemia-reperfusion causing vasodilation, myocardial dysfunction, coagulopathy, and neuroexcitotoxicity. Needs ICU support.
- **Pseudo-PEA:** You diagnose asystole but POCUS shows weak ventricular contraction. Underlying cause (tamponade, tension pneumothorax, hypovolaemia) must be treated or rhythm becomes truly pulseless.
- **Late presentation patterns (exam stems):**
  - "Unresponsive with monitor showing asystole, but ZOLL/ultrasound shows fine VF" → you missed fine pVT → shock immediately.
  - "Post-resuscitation limb warmth and loss of consciousness" → consider spinal cord injury vs cerebral oedema; if ICP >20 mmHg, worsen prognosis.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

- **"4H & 4T"** – Reversible causes to check for persistent pVT:
  - Hypoxia, Hypovolaemia, Hypo/hyperkalaemia, Hypothermia
  - Tension pneumothorax, Tamponade (cardiac), Toxins, Thrombosis (coronary/pulmonary)
- **"SHOCK"** – Management priority:
  - S – Shock immediately (defibrillate)
  - H – Heart it (Chest compressions)
  - O – Oxygenate (bag-mask ventilation after 30 compressions)
  - C – Correct causes (4H&4T)
  - K – Kill it again (repeat shocks)

**High-yield pearls:**
1. **"Shock first, ask questions later"** – When in wide-complex arrest, never waste time checking pulse for >10 seconds; if rhythm suggests pVT/VF, shock.
2. **"Fine pVT is not asystole"** – If amplitude <2 mm, increase gain or switch to lead II/V5. If any organized pattern, treat as shockable.
3. **"Torsades = magnesium, not amiodarone"** – Giving amiodarone in long QT syndrome can worsen arrhythmia (prolongs QT further).
4. **"One shock, then 2 minutes CPR"** – Do not deliver stacked shocks (except for witnessed arrest with advanced airway and continuous monitoring in some protocols). Modern biphasic defibrillators have >90% first-shock success – wait for rhythm check.
5. **"After ROSC, don't forget the ECG"** – Look for ischaemia; immediate PCI improves survival in shockable arrests.
6. **"Pre-arrest clue: hypokalemia + digoxin toxicity"** – Classic cause of pVT; treat with digoxin Fab, K+ replacement.
7. **"Pulseless VT in young? Think HCM or commotio"** – Obtain family history, echocardiogram, and genetic testing.

**Common mistakes to avoid:**
- Correcting hyperkalaemia with calcium gluconate only if K+ >6.5 or ECG changes (wide QRS, sine wave) – calcium stabilises membrane.
- Not defibrillating in pregnancy – safe; left lateral tilt for compressions.
- Continuing to shock pVT that degenerates into PEA – switch to IV adrenaline/atropine (for PEA) and treat underlying cause.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
- **When to call crash team / senior immediately:**
  - Any pulseless arrest – press emergency button or call 2222 (UK)/local code blue.
  - After first 3 shocks if no ROSC → call anaesthetics/intensivist for airway and further management.
- **When to involve cardiology:**
  - ROSC achieved but unstable rhythm or ST elevation → urgent PCI team activation.
  - Recurrent pVT (≥2 episodes in 24h) → cardiology / electrophysiology consultation for ICD consideration.
- **Specific thresholds for ICU:**
  - Post-ROSC GCS ≤8 → intubate and ventilate; protective lung strategy.
  - MAP <65 mmHg despite fluid resuscitation → start noradrenaline (0.05-0.5 μg/kg/min IV infusion).
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L after arrest → indicates ongoing tissue hypoperfusion; aim for clearance within 12h.
  - Temperature >37.8°C → start targeted temperature management (cooling to 32-36°C for 24h).
  - Blood glucose >10 mmol/L → insulin infusion protocol.
- **When to call ethics / transplant team:**
  - No ROSC after 20 minutes of ALS and no reversible cause → consider terminal event; discuss family and possible organ donation (if appropriate).

rawMarkdown:
## Pulseless Ventricular Tachycardia (pVT)
**One-liner:** Shockable cardiac arrest rhythm from disorganized ventricular electrical activity; occurs post-MI, in cardiomyopathy, or channelopathies; requires immediate defibrillation.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See
**Classic vignette:** A 62-year-old man, 3 days post-anterior ST-elevation MI, collapses on the ward. Nursing staff find him unresponsive, not breathing, and no pulse. The cardiac monitor initially shows a run of wide-complex tachycardia at 200/min, then degenerates into a coarse, sinusoidal waveform. Resuscitation trolley arrives; rhythm check reveals no output. He had complained of chest tightness and palpitations 20 minutes earlier.

**Dissecting the vignette:**
- **Post-MI setting** → scarred myocardium creates re-entry circuits; highest risk in first 48-72 hours
- **Collapse and no pulse** → hemodynamically unstable; must distinguish from perfusing VT (which has a pulse)
- **Wide-complex tachycardia before arrest** → ventricular origin; QRS >120 ms, no visible P waves
- **Coarse sinusoidal waveform** → characteristic pattern of pVT; amplitude often >5 mm
- **Chest tightness and palpitations** → prodomal ischemia triggers ectopy; may herald VT storm

**Atypical presentations:**
- **Torsades de Pointes (TdP):** Polymorphic pVT with twisting QRS axis; often drug-induced (e.g., sotalol), hypomagnesemia, or congenital long QT. On monitor: sinusoidal amplitude waxes and wanes.
- **Fine pVT:** Low-amplitude, almost flat line → easily mistaken for asystole. Switch leads or gain; look for slow, irregular undulations. Must treat as pVT until proven otherwise.
- **pVT in young athlete:** No MI history; suspect hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, myocarditis, or commotio cordis (blow to chest). Presentation: sudden collapse during exertion.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)
**Plain-language definition:** Pulseless ventricular tachycardia is a rapid, organized ventricular rhythm (≥120 bpm) that fails to generate a palpable pulse. The heart contracts too fast and ineffectively → no cardiac output → cardiac arrest.

**Why it matters:**
- **Frequency:** ~20% of in-hospital cardiac arrests are shockable rhythms (pVT or VF). pVT is the most common initial rhythm in out-of-hospital cardiac arrest with known cardiac disease.
- **Stakes:** Defibrillation is the only effective treatment; each minute delay reduces survival by 7-10%. Mortality >85% without prompt intervention.
- **Common pitfalls:**
  - Mistaking pVT for asystole (fine pVT) → delays shocking → worse outcome.
  - Trying to check a pulse during rhythm check → waste time; if no pulse → shock.
  - Forgetting to treat underlying cause (e.g., acute coronary syndrome, electrolyte disturbance) → recurrence.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings
**Story of development:**
1. **Trigger:** A premature ventricular complex (PVC) falls on the vulnerable period of repolarization (R-on-T phenomenon). This initiates a re-entry circuit around scar tissue, ischemia border zone, or electrolyte imbalance.
2. **Re-entry:** A unidirectional block in one limb of the circuit allows the wavefront to circle back and re-excite tissue. This self-perpetuating loop causes rapid ventricular firing (150-250 bpm).
3. **Loss of output:** At such high rates, ventricular filling time collapses → stroke volume drops → no pulse. The atria continue to contract but against a closed AV valve → no atrial kick.

**Mechanism → sign:**
- **Rapid wide QRS** → ventricular origin; conduction bypasses His-Purkinje system → slow cell-to-cell conduction → broad QRS (usually >140 ms)
- **No P waves** → sinus node cannot compete; sometimes retrograde P waves buried in QRS or T wave (telltale sign if visible)
- **Monomorphic pVT** → single re-entry circuit (e.g., around MI scar); QRS shape constant
- **Polymorphic pVT** → multiple circuits or changing substrate (e.g., ischemia, electrolyte flux); QRS morphology varies beat-to-beat; think TdP if twisting axis
- **Coarse vs fine** → amplitude reflects mass of contracting myocardium; coarse means more viable muscle – more likely to respond to defibrillation

**Connection to pattern cues:**
- Post-MI vignette → re-entry around scar
- Young athlete → structural heart disease (HCM) or blunt trauma (commotio) causes ventricular irritability
- Torsades → triggered activity due to prolonged repolarization (long QT) – not re-entry

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Common Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|--------------|-----------|
| **Ischaemic** | Acute MI (STEMI > NSTEMI) | Chest pain, ST elevation, troponin rise |
| **Structural** | Cardiomyopathy (ischaemic, dilated, hypertrophic) | Known LV dysfunction, heart failure |
| **Electrolyte** | Hypokalaemia, hypomagnesaemia, hyperkalaemia | Diuretic use, renal failure, prolonged QT |
| **Drug/toxin** | Antiarrhythmics (IC – flecainide, III – sotalol/amiodarone) | Induction of torsades; preceding PVCs |
| **Channelopathy** | Long QT syndrome, Brugada syndrome | Family history of sudden death, syncope |
| **Electrical** | Commotio cordis, electrocution | Blunt chest trauma (sports), electrical injury |
| **Inflammatory** | Myocarditis (viral, autoimmune) | Fever, recent viral illness, chest pain |
| **Other** | Hypothermia, hypoxia, acidosis | Environmental exposure, sepsis, respiratory failure |

**Most common cause:** Acute myocardial infarction (especially anterior STEMI) – responsible for ~50-70% of pVT arrests.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means
**Diagnostic criteria:** pVT is defined by: absence of pulse + monomorphic or polymorphic wide QRS tachycardia (≥120 bpm) on cardiac monitor or ECG. No further scoring needed; it's a rhythm diagnosis in cardiac arrest.

**Key tests (all during arrest or immediately post-ROSC):**
- **Rhythm check (paddles/monitor):** Shows wide QRS >120 bpm, no pulse. **Single finding confirms diagnosis** – proceed to defibrillation.
- **12-lead ECG (post-ROSC):** Look for ischaemia (ST elevation/depression), QT prolongation, Brugada pattern, or evidence of underlying structural disease. **Pattern:** If QT >500 ms in sinus rhythm, suspect TdP risk.
- **Arterial blood gas (ABG):** Assess oxygenation, acidosis, potassium, magnesium. **Interpretation:** Severe acidosis (pH <7.0) impairs defibrillation success; correct K+ <3.5 or >5.5, Mg <1.8 mg/dL (0.74 mmol/L).
- **Troponin:** Post-arrest – elevated due to ischaemia (either cause or consequence). Serial trend helps distinguish primary MI vs arrest-related damage.
- **Point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS):** During pulse check – if rhythm looks like pVT but POCUS shows coordinated ventricular contraction → pseudo-PEA (treat underlying cause). **Rules out pVT if organized contraction seen.**
- **ECG during stable VT (if perfusing):** For structurally normal hearts, VT can be idiopathic (RVOT, fascicular). But if pulseless → treat as pVT regardless.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning
**Immediate (first hour):** Follow ALS algorithm.
- **Call for help and defibrillator.** Start CPR with hard and fast compressions (100-120/min, depth 5-6 cm), 30:2 ratio.
- **Defibrillate** *as soon as rhythm confirmed pVT.* Use biphasic 150-200 J (monophasic 360 J). If unsure pVT vs asystole → give shock (low risk, high benefit).
- **After each shock:** Immediately resume CPR for 2 min, then rhythm check. Do not delay CPR to check pulse.
- **If shock fails (refractory pVT):**
  - **Amiodarone 300 mg IV/IO** (first dose) followed by 5% dextrose flush. After 3-5 shocks if still pVT → second dose amiodarone 150 mg IV/IO.
  - Alternative: **Lidocaine 1-1.5 mg/kg IV/IO** (max 3 mg/kg). Use if amiodarone contraindicated (e.g., known thyroid disease, prolonged QT on prior ECG).
  - **Magnesium 2 g IV/IO** if Torsades de Pointes suspected (polymorphic pVT with long QT on pre-arrest ECG).
- **Treat reversible causes (4H & 4T):** Hypoxia, Hypovolaemia, Hypo/hyperkalaemia, Hypothermia; Tension pneumothorax, Tamponade, Toxins, Thrombosis (coronary/pulmonary). These explain persistent pVT despite shocks.
- **If return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC):** Post-cardiac arrest care – controlled oxygenation, target MAP >65 mmHg, therapeutic hypothermia, urgent PCI if STEMI.

**Ward (24-72h):**
- **Continuous ECG monitoring** in CCU/ICU. Watch for recurrent pVT or VT storm.
- **Correct electrolytes:** K+ >4.0, Mg >1.0 mmol/L, Ca++ adjusted normal.
- **Identify and treat acute coronary syndrome:** Urgent angiography ± revascularisation.
- **If frequent PVCs/short runs:** consider beta-blocker (e.g., metoprolol 50 mg PO BD) to suppress triggers.
- **Amiodarone loading:** 200 mg PO TDS for 1 week, then 200 mg daily for maintenance (only if not already given IV loading).

**Long-term:**
- **ICD implantation** (secondary prevention) in survivors with structural heart disease (LVEF ≤35%) or genetic channelopathies.
- **Optimise heart failure therapy** (ACEi, beta-blocker, spironolactone) to reduce arrhythmia recurrence.
- **Avoid pro-arrhythmic drugs** (especially class IC agents like flecainide in structural disease).
- **Education:** Patient & family – when to call 999/emergency services for palpitations or syncope.

**Common pitfalls:**
- **Do NOT** give vasopressin (ineffective) or amiodarine after asystole/PEA (no benefit, may lower BP).
- **Do NOT** delay shock to intubate or get IV access. Shock first, then access.
- **Do NOT** use paddle-to-skin contact with gel – use defibrillator pads correctly to avoid burns.
- **Do NOT** stop CPR for more than 5-10 seconds during rhythm check.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Pulseless VT | Ventricular Fibrillation (VF) | Asystole | Perfusing VT (pVT with pulse) |
|---------|--------------|-------------------------------|----------|------------------------------|
| **Pulse** | Absent | Absent | Absent | Present (weak) |
| **ECG (rhythm)** | Wide QRS complexes (regular) | Chaotic, no clear QRS | Flat line | Wide QRS, regular, rate 100-150 |
| **Amplitude** | Coarse (≥2 mm) or fine | Usually coarse; fine VF <2 mm | No amplitude | Variable |
| **Tachycardia** | Yes (≥120 bpm) | Can be rapid but unorganised | No | Yes (120-200 bpm) |
| **QRS morphology** | Monomorphic or polymorphic | None (fibrillatory waves) | None | Monomorphic/polymorphic |
| **Treatment** | Defibrillate, amiodarone | Defibrillate, amiodarone | CPR, adrenaline (epinephrine) | If unstable → cardioversion; if stable → amiodarone/electrophysiology |

**Key differentiator:** Perfusing VT has a pulse – check central pulse (carotid/femoral) for up to 10 seconds. If unsure, treat as pulseless.

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?
- **Failure to shock immediately:** Account for each minute delay → doubling of mortality. After 10 minutes, survival <2%. Brain injury from prolonged hypoxia.
- **Recurrent pVT storm:** Multiple shocks may cause myocardial stunning, hypotension, and electrical myocardial depression. Management: sedation (propofol), revascularisation, deep sedation with beta-blocker ± overdrive pacing.
- **Post-arrest syndrome:** Global ischemia-reperfusion causing vasodilation, myocardial dysfunction, coagulopathy, and neuroexcitotoxicity. Needs ICU support.
- **Pseudo-PEA:** You diagnose asystole but POCUS shows weak ventricular contraction. Underlying cause (tamponade, tension pneumothorax, hypovolaemia) must be treated or rhythm becomes truly pulseless.
- **Late presentation patterns (exam stems):**
  - "Unresponsive with monitor showing asystole, but ZOLL/ultrasound shows fine VF" → you missed fine pVT → shock immediately.
  - "Post-resuscitation limb warmth and loss of consciousness" → consider spinal cord injury vs cerebral oedema; if ICP >20 mmHg, worsen prognosis.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls
**Mnemonics:**

- **"4H & 4T"** – Reversible causes to check for persistent pVT:
  - Hypoxia, Hypovolaemia, Hypo/hyperkalaemia, Hypothermia
  - Tension pneumothorax, Tamponade (cardiac), Toxins, Thrombosis (coronary/pulmonary)
- **"SHOCK"** – Management priority:
  - S – Shock immediately (defibrillate)
  - H – Heart it (Chest compressions)
  - O – Oxygenate (bag-mask ventilation after 30 compressions)
  - C – Correct causes (4H&4T)
  - K – Kill it again (repeat shocks)

**High-yield pearls:**
1. **"Shock first, ask questions later"** – When in wide-complex arrest, never waste time checking pulse for >10 seconds; if rhythm suggests pVT/VF, shock.
2. **"Fine pVT is not asystole"** – If amplitude <2 mm, increase gain or switch to lead II/V5. If any organized pattern, treat as shockable.
3. **"Torsades = magnesium, not amiodarone"** – Giving amiodarone in long QT syndrome can worsen arrhythmia (prolongs QT further).
4. **"One shock, then 2 minutes CPR"** – Do not deliver stacked shocks (except for witnessed arrest with advanced airway and continuous monitoring in some protocols). Modern biphasic defibrillators have >90% first-shock success – wait for rhythm check.
5. **"After ROSC, don't forget the ECG"** – Look for ischaemia; immediate PCI improves survival in shockable arrests.
6. **"Pre-arrest clue: hypokalemia + digoxin toxicity"** – Classic cause of pVT; treat with digoxin Fab, K+ replacement.
7. **"Pulseless VT in young? Think HCM or commotio"** – Obtain family history, echocardiogram, and genetic testing.

**Common mistakes to avoid:**
- Correcting hyperkalaemia with calcium gluconate only if K+ >6.5 or ECG changes (wide QRS, sine wave) – calcium stabilises membrane.
- Not defibrillating in pregnancy – safe; left lateral tilt for compressions.
- Continuing to shock pVT that degenerates into PEA – switch to IV adrenaline/atropine (for PEA) and treat underlying cause.

### 10. Escalation Criteria
- **When to call crash team / senior immediately:**
  - Any pulseless arrest – press emergency button or call 2222 (UK)/local code blue.
  - After first 3 shocks if no ROSC → call anaesthetics/intensivist for airway and further management.
- **When to involve cardiology:**
  - ROSC achieved but unstable rhythm or ST elevation → urgent PCI team activation.
  - Recurrent pVT (≥2 episodes in 24h) → cardiology / electrophysiology consultation for ICD consideration.
- **Specific thresholds for ICU:**
  - Post-ROSC GCS ≤8 → intubate and ventilate; protective lung strategy.
  - MAP <65 mmHg despite fluid resuscitation → start noradrenaline (0.05-0.5 μg/kg/min IV infusion).
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L after arrest → indicates ongoing tissue hypoperfusion; aim for clearance within 12h.
  - Temperature >37.8°C → start targeted temperature management (cooling to 32-36°C for 24h).
  - Blood glucose >10 mmol/L → insulin infusion protocol.
- **When to call ethics / transplant team:**
  - No ROSC after 20 minutes of ALS and no reversible cause → consider terminal event; discuss family and possible organ donation (if appropriate).
